title: Discovering state-of-the-art reinforcement learning algorithms

data_scope.external_data: None (explicitly stated: 'No external data were used')
data_scope.held_out_benchmarks: Crafter, NetHack, Sokoban (never seen during discovery)
data_scope.steps_per_game: approximately 600 million environment steps per Atari game

core_conclusion: Machines can autonomously discover state-of-the-art reinforcement learning algorithms that outperform all manually designed rules. The meta-learned DiscoRL rule surpassed every existing RL rule on the Atari benchmark (IQM 13.86, exceeding MuZero) and generalised to held-out benchmarks (ProcGen, Crafter, NetHack, Sokoban) it never encountered during discovery. Performance and generality improve as more diverse environments are used, suggesting future RL algorithms for advanced AI may be machine-discovered rather than manually designed.
why_it_matters: First machine-discovered RL rule to beat human-designed state of the art; strong zero-shot generalisation across six held-out benchmarks without domain knowledge; performance scales with environment diversity and compute; discovers semantically novel prediction targets distinct from value functions; full open-source release of meta-parameters and code.
why_nature: Paradigm-shifting result of broad scientific significance; rigorous large-scale empirical validation across six established benchmarks with statistical confidence intervals; biological resonance (evolution-discovered RL analogy); direct relevance to AI safety and capability discourse around self-improving AI systems.
added_value_to_my_research: DiscoRL meta-learning framework applied to spatial accessibility domains: discovers optimal 2SFCA/R2SFCA catchment rules instead of hand-tuned distance-decay parameters
DiscoRL's discovered prediction vectors (y, z) as self-supervised spatiotemporal embeddings for urban health and mobility prediction models
Meta-learned multi-objective RL rule for facility-location optimization (EV charging, healthcare siting) that generalizes across city types without manual objective engineering
Distributional extension of DiscoRL's meta-network for uncertainty-aware spatial accessibility, producing full access distributions rather than point estimates for equity policy
Diversity-scaling insight: curating geographically diverse training cities (not just more data) is the principled strategy for cross-city transfer in geospatial foundation model fine-tuning

related.title: Efficient and scalable reinforcement learning for large-scale network control
related.relation: scalable RL for complex network control — complementary application domain for meta-learned RL rules

related.title: Multi-timescale reinforcement learning in the brain
related.relation: biological multi-timescale RL — connects to DiscoRL's temporal prediction semantics and bootstrapping mechanism

related.title: An opponent striatal circuit for distributional reinforcement learning
related.relation: distributional RL in biological circuits — natural extension of DiscoRL's discoverable prediction target space

related.title: Automating turbulence modelling by multi-agent reinforcement learning
related.relation: automating domain-expert rules with multi-agent RL — parallel to DiscoRL automating RL algorithm design

related.title: Algorithmic fairness and bias mitigation for clinical machine learning with deep reinforcement learning
related.relation: applying deep RL to optimise fairness objectives — connects to meta-learned equity-aware decision rules

figures.caption: The DiscoRL discovery pipeline: population of agents in diverse environments, agent architecture with policy/prediction outputs, LSTM meta-network producing targets, and meta-gradient optimization loop
DiscoRL performance vs. human-designed RL rules (MuZero, PPO, Dreamer, IMPALA, Rainbow) on Atari, ProcGen, DMLab, Crafter, NetHack, and Sokoban — Disco103 improves on every benchmark
Discovery efficiency (best rule within ~3 agent lifetimes per game), scalability with number of environments, and ablation study of architectural components

Analysis of DiscoRL's discovered predictions: saliency spikes before rewards, gradient-based attention maps, information content R² scores, bootstrapping horizon analysis, and ablation of prediction access
